one_line_takeaway: Shared-state LLM agents can silently corrupt one user's answers with another benign user's scope-bound conventions at 57-71% rates with no attacker involved, and text-level write-time sanitization only fixes the conversational case -- executable stored artifacts need artifact-level, provenance-aware defenses.
key_finding: Under raw shared state (no defense), benign interactions alone produce high contamination rates: 59.6% overall on MIMIC-III, 70.7% on eICU, and 57.4% on Slack (57-71% range). The dominant contamination type is mechanism-dependent: in EHRAgent, SC and TC lead (up to 88.9% SC on eICU, 68.9% TC on MIMIC-III) because those conventions localize into structured record fields, while PC is lower; in Slack the pattern reverses (SC 83.3%, PC 66.7% high; TC only 20% because formatting rarely alters correctness of plain-text messaging). Write-time sanitization (SSI) works well ONLY when shared state is purely conversational: Slack 57%->6% (near elimination). When shared state contains executable artifacts (EHRAgent solution code), SSI leaves substantial residual risk: eICU 71%->33% and MIMIC-III only 60%->41%. The mechanism is that SSI removes the convention's TEXT but the convention survives inside stored solution code, which EHRAgent retrieves and reuses as a template. Localized conventions (TC/SC, e.g. a ROUND call or a WHERE clause) are in principle amenable to code-level fixes, but pervasive procedural (PC) conventions shape the whole solution structure and resist even hypothetical code-level sanitization (PC barely moves: MIMIC-III 44%->41%, eICU 64%->50%). Failure-mode analysis shows the safety-critical concern: eICU contaminations manifest as wrong answers 98% of the time under raw state (silent failures), whereas MIMIC-III's more complex schema (17 tables, >50% of queries needing 3+ joins) breaks contaminated code, yielding 41% detectable no-answer failures.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes 'unintentional cross-user contamination' (UCC) as a distinct robustness failure mode in shared-state LLM agents that serve multiple users from one persistent knowledge layer (memory bank or conversational context). UCC's defining property is that there is NO attacker: a benign source interaction leaves behind a locally-valid but scope-bound artifact (e.g., a redefined term, a rounding/formatting rule, or a workflow choice) that the agent later misapplies to a different user's request, silently degrading that user's outcome. The authors' own contributions are (1) a shared-state agent model with explicit write/read/agent-function operations and a controlled clean-vs-contaminated evaluation protocol; (2) a three-way taxonomy of contamination effects -- semantic (SC), transformation (TC), and procedural (PC); (3) empirical demonstration of high contamination rates from purely benign interactions across two shared-state mechanisms; and (4) a write-time defense, Sanitized Shared Interaction (SSI), which they position as a practical baseline, not a complete solution.
threat_model: Explicitly NON-adversarial -- the paper's title is 'No Attacker Needed' and it repeatedly distinguishes UCC from prior attacker-driven work (indirect prompt injection, memory poisoning such as MINJA/Dong et al. 2025, and cross-user attacks such as MURMUR/Patlan et al. 2025, which are cited as related work, not this paper's contribution). The 'threat' is a system failure model: a single agent serves multiple users (a team/workspace/org) over time via persistent shared state; one benign user's scope-bound convention persists and is retrieved/surfaced when serving a later 'victim' user whose correct answer differs. The source user has no hidden instructions, triggers, or optimized harmful objective. The harm is loss of correctness/task utility for the later user, often as a silent wrong answer. The authors do NOT design or evaluate any attack.
limitations: Scale is small and hand-constructed: only 34 manually-designed source conventions across all three datasets, each with up to 3 victim tasks and 3 trials, so reported rates rest on a limited number of instances (e.g. Slack's 20% TC is over only 3 TC conventions). SSI is explicitly a text-only write-time baseline that does not touch the read path, retrieval, or reasoning, and the authors concede any contamination surviving sanitization propagates unchecked; they do not implement the code-level or provenance-aware defenses they propose. Everything is evaluated with a single backbone (GPT-4o at temperature 0); no ablation across models or temperatures. The MURMUR contamination judgment relies on GPT-4o LLM-as-a-judge, which may have its own error/bias. Victim tasks are pre-filtered to be clean and constructed to be semantically similar to the source so retrieval of the source entry is 'expected by construction,' which likely inflates contamination rates relative to organic deployments. The two environments are both fairly narrow (clinical SQL/code generation and a Slack simulation).

datasets_used: MIMIC-III (clinical EHR database; Johnson et al. 2016) via EHRAgent
eICU (eICU Collaborative Research Database; Pollard et al. 2018) via EHRAgent
Slack workspace domain from the MURMUR framework (Patlan et al. 2025)
quantitative_results: Raw contamination rate overall: MIMIC-III 59.6%, eICU 70.7%, Slack 57.4%
Raw MIMIC-III by type: SC 59.3%, TC 68.9%, PC 44.4%
Raw eICU by type: SC 88.9%, TC 66.7%, PC 63.6%
Raw Slack by type: SC 83.3%, TC 20.0%, PC 66.7%
SSI reduces overall: Slack 57%->6%, eICU 71%->33%, MIMIC-III 60%->41%
SSI on eICU TC: 67%->7% (near elimination); MIMIC-III TC only 69%->47%
PC most resistant to SSI: MIMIC-III 44%->41%, eICU 64%->50%
Failure modes (raw): eICU 98% wrong-answer / 2% no-answer; MIMIC-III 59% wrong-answer / 41% no-answer
Failure modes (SSI): eICU 84% wrong / 16% no-answer; MIMIC-III 59% / 41%
34 source conventions total (MIMIC-III 11, eICU 14, Slack 9); 3 independent trials each
EHRAgent retrieval: top-4 entries, all-MiniLM-L6-v2 embeddings, memory bank 4 seeds -> 5 after source
MIMIC-III schema complexity cited as driver: 17 tables, 2.7 tables/query, >50% of queries need 3+ joins
Case study: PC 'absolute value' convention makes agent return 26 (max-min range) instead of correct signed change -1
baselines_compared: Raw shared state (no defense) -- the primary comparison condition
SSI (Sanitized Shared Interaction) -- the authors' own proposed write-time sanitizer, evaluated as a practical baseline
No external competing defense methods are benchmarked; MINJA (Dong et al. 2025) and MURMUR (Patlan et al. 2025) are cited as related attacker-driven work, not run as baselines
